caveats: a: No public occupation mix is available specifically for 532490, and the equipment categories have different service and logistics intensity.
a: BLS occupational pages describe maintenance and material-handling work across industries rather than wage-weighted rental-company tasks.
a: Large public operators may have more centralized systems and existing automation than lower-middle-market firms.
rho: No representative source measures AI adoption or labor realization in 532490.
rho: Connected-fleet evidence from construction-equipment rental is adjacent to, but excluded from, the exact NAICS code.
rho: Benefits may improve utilization and uptime without reducing labor.

e: The frozen firm count uses a round 30% margin because no clean rental-and-leasing margin exists; equipment mix, leverage, depreciation, and ancillary services can materially change the implied band.
e: NAICS 532490 spans modular buildings, electronic test gear, medical equipment, pallets, theatrical assets, agricultural machines, and industrial equipment.
e: Asset condition and debt can make nominally qualifying firms unsuitable despite recurring revenue.
s5: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying control sales.
s5: The proxy does not capture asset-backed debt, fleet appraisal, or rental-sector consolidation.
s5: Strategic buyers may prefer asset purchases or branch tuck-ins rather than whole-company control transfers.
q: No representative source measures automation-benefit retention in 532490.
q: Daily transactional rentals, multi-month leases, public-sector contracts, and negotiated enterprise accounts have different repricing behavior.
q: Utilization gains can require additional fleet investment and physical service capacity.
d5: One large diversified operator does not represent the national 532490 population.
d5: Reported rental revenue combines price, mix, fleet growth, utilization, and service fees rather than constant-quality quantity.
d5: Demand can diverge sharply across modular, agricultural, medical, theatrical, telecommunications, and test equipment.
o: Operator requirement does not guarantee that a standalone LMM firm retains the relationship; manufacturers and national platforms can integrate rental operations.
o: Low-touch equipment such as pallets can be more digitally managed than modular buildings or calibrated test equipment.
o: The adjacent connected-fleet example may overstate digital displacement for less connected categories.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Other Commercial and Industrial Machinery and Equipment Rental and Leasing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Businesses, institutions, schools, farms, producers, and service providers pay recurring or project-based daily, monthly, or term rental charges plus delivery, installation, modification, maintenance, damage-waiver, pickup, and other ancillary fees; operators also sell used fleet assets and sometimes new equipment.
lens.excluded_activities: Heavy construction, off-highway transportation, mining, and forestry equipment rental; office equipment rental; equipment supplied with operators; finance leases combined with buyer loans; consumer and home-use rentals; captive fleets, shells, non-control financing situations, and firms outside the target normalized-EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms repeatedly renting or leasing nonconsumer commercial and industrial equipment without operators, including manufacturing, metalworking, telecommunications, motion-picture, theatrical, service-industry, institutional-furniture, agricultural, medical, modular-building, scaffolding, pallet, and industrial-truck equipment within NAICS 532490.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: official 532490 equipment scope, a diversified B2B rental operator's disclosed workflows and costs, and BLS descriptions of industrial machinery maintenance and material-moving tasks. Transformation: mapped quoting, records, contracts, billing, support, utilization, diagnostics, scheduling, and documentation to exposed hours while assigning low exposure to cleaning, handling, transport, installation, repair, calibration, and safety work. Error direction: public-company centralization and broad occupational sources may overstate digital exposure at small branches, while unmeasured administrative work performed by sales and technicians may understate it.
caveats: No public occupation mix is available specifically for 532490, and the equipment categories have different service and logistics intensity.
BLS occupational pages describe maintenance and material-handling work across industries rather than wage-weighted rental-company tasks.
Large public operators may have more centralized systems and existing automation than lower-middle-market firms.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and time study from 532490 operators, split by equipment category and branch function and distinguishing current software and telematics from new AI capability, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Quoting, availability matching, scheduling, contract generation, credit checks, customer service, billing, collections, utilization analysis, maintenance planning, parts lookup, inspection documentation, and sales support are exposed to AI assistance. Fleet handling, cleaning, delivery, installation, repair, calibration, safety inspection, and physical refurbishment remain labor-intensive.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: McGrath RentCorp reported rental operations revenue of $612.5 million in 2023, $638.4 million in 2024, and $665.6 million in 2025 across modular, storage, and electronic-test rental businesses. Transformation: treated the observed recurring-rental growth as a directional anchor, moderated it over five years, and widened the range for pricing, fleet mix, end-market cycles, and category heterogeneity. Error direction: nominal revenue and public-company fleet expansion can overstate constant-price demand, while broader adoption of rental instead of ownership could make the low case conservative.
caveats: One large diversified operator does not represent the national 532490 population.
Reported rental revenue combines price, mix, fleet growth, utilization, and service fees rather than constant-quality quantity.
Demand can diverge sharply across modular, agricultural, medical, theatrical, telecommunications, and test equipment.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-price series of rental days, units on rent, and active customers for 532490 establishments, split by equipment class and excluding fleet price and mix effects, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Renting remains attractive when customers need temporary capacity, specialized assets, technological flexibility, or avoidance of ownership and maintenance. A diversified operator reported rental operations revenue rising from $612.5 million in 2023 to $665.6 million in 2025, while category demand varied. Five-year growth is tempered by industrial and construction cycles, interest rates, customer capital spending, equipment life, supply, and substitution between renting and ownership.

caveats: The frozen firm count uses a round 30% margin because no clean rental-and-leasing margin exists; equipment mix, leverage, depreciation, and ancillary services can materially change the implied band.
NAICS 532490 spans modular buildings, electronic test gear, medical equipment, pallets, theatrical assets, agricultural machines, and industrial equipment.
Asset condition and debt can make nominally qualifying firms unsuitable despite recurring revenue.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level dataset covering fleet original cost and age, debt, utilization, rental versus sales mix, normalized EBITDA, customer and supplier concentration, maintenance records, branch density, ownership, and transferable contracts would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Recurring external rental is intrinsic to the industry and many regional specialists can be transferable. Eligibility is reduced by the margin-bridged firm-count uncertainty, insufficient normalized earnings, aging or obsolete fleet, excessive leverage, manufacturer dependence, customer or end-market concentration, weak maintenance records, owner relationships, poor utilization, environmental or safety liabilities, and fragmented systems.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 532490 ($6,857,450,042) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 532490 ($42,047,695,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3000 (margins.json[532]: ESTIMATE: rental & leasing is asset-heavy with no clean Damodaran bucket (REITs 43.79% overstates operating rental); 30% chosen as a round mid-point before deriving results); revenue/employee $357,239 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 532490); class <5: 2,787 firms, avg revenue/firm $569,384 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $170,815 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 778 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,920,087 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $576,026 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 568 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,888,530 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,166,559 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 569 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,670,112 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,501,034 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 162 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,386,216 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,615,865 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 108 firms, avg revenue/firm $225,791,194 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $67,737,358 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,137

o.bridge: Source population/quantity: official equipment scope, disclosed rental asset-management and service workflows, BLS physical maintenance and handling duties, and connected-fleet technology in adjacent equipment rental. Transformation: assigned high operator need to capital ownership, storage, inspection, cleaning, repair, calibration, delivery, installation, safety, and residual management, while discounting for digital marketplaces, remote monitoring, manufacturer-direct programs, and customer ownership. Error direction: current physical-service requirements may overstate independent-operator retention if platforms vertically integrate, while safety, maintenance, logistics, and capital needs may keep the required share higher.
caveats: Operator requirement does not guarantee that a standalone LMM firm retains the relationship; manufacturers and national platforms can integrate rental operations.
Low-touch equipment such as pallets can be more digitally managed than modular buildings or calibrated test equipment.
The adjacent connected-fleet example may overstate digital displacement for less connected categories.
o.change_evidence: Customer-cohort data comparing traditional rental firms with peer-to-peer, manufacturer-direct, marketplace-only, and ownership channels, including uptime, delivery, maintenance, safety, total cost, and repeat use, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Digital marketplaces and customer-owned or manufacturer-managed fleets can bypass some independent rental branches, but software does not replace ownership or financing of physical assets, availability management, inspection, cleaning, maintenance, repair, storage, delivery, installation, pickup, safety accountability, and residual-value management. Most year-five quantity therefore still needs an accountable equipment-rental operator.

caveats: No representative source measures automation-benefit retention in 532490.
Daily transactional rentals, multi-month leases, public-sector contracts, and negotiated enterprise accounts have different repricing behavior.
Utilization gains can require additional fleet investment and physical service capacity.
q.change_evidence: Matched contract, rate, utilization, labor, maintenance, logistics, capital-cost, renewal, and contribution-margin data before and after automation across equipment categories would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Rental rates and ancillary fees can preserve workflow savings during contract terms, while higher utilization and uptime may add capacity without immediate price cuts. Retention is limited by competitive quoting, project bids, renewals, national-account bargaining, service expectations, fuel and logistics charges, equipment financing, manufacturer costs, and the need to reinvest savings in fleet, maintenance, and customer response.

caveats: No representative source measures AI adoption or labor realization in 532490.
Connected-fleet evidence from construction-equipment rental is adjacent to, but excluded from, the exact NAICS code.
Benefits may improve utilization and uptime without reducing labor.
rho.change_evidence: Pre/post deployment evidence from multi-branch 532490 operators covering administrative and technician hours, quote conversion, utilization, maintenance cost, downtime, damage, safety events, billing errors, and implementation cost would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years permits AI-enabled quoting, customer communications, document processing, collections prioritization, predictive-maintenance support, parts lookup, utilization optimization, and technician copilots. Realization is constrained by fragmented fleets and systems, sensor coverage, branch practices, data quality, safety, customer-specific configurations, field connectivity, technician acceptance, integration costs, and the need to validate recommendations on physical assets.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup's fall-2024 survey reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners plan to sell, transfer, or take public within five years. Transformation: used 22% as an upper anchor and discounted for noncompletion, family and internal transfers, fleet and debt diligence, lender consent, customer concentration, asset obsolescence, environmental and safety issues, and non-control asset deals. Error direction: broad owner intentions overstate completed qualifying control transfers, while ongoing rental-industry consolidation could make the lower bound conservative.
caveats: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying control sales.
The proxy does not capture asset-backed debt, fleet appraisal, or rental-sector consolidation.
Strategic buyers may prefer asset purchases or branch tuck-ins rather than whole-company control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Closed control-sale counts and eligible-firm denominators from equipment-rental brokers, association transaction data, lender collateral records, and state UCC or merger filings over a five-year cohort would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful five-year sale or transfer intentions, and rental fleets, branches, customer books, and maintenance capabilities can support control transfers. Completion is discounted for noncompletion, internal transfers, debt and lender consents, fleet valuation, environmental and safety diligence, owner dependence, customer concentration, and equipment obsolescence.

principal_driver: The principal driver is combining recurring rental relationships with AI-assisted quoting, scheduling, support, billing, utilization, and maintenance planning.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is that value is tied to capital-intensive, category-specific physical fleets whose debt, utilization, maintenance, and residual risks cannot be automated away.
